author: @paulcrouchley6289
text:
Brilliant turbine.
Built one a few years ago from a old computer hard drive!
Disks/platters are all there and perfectly balanced. Drilled holes around center of disks for exhaust air, inserted an air nozzle through the casing and built up the casing with filler for a snug fit around the disks.
No way of measuring rpm but sound passed up through a shrill scream and went beyond my audible range in a matter of seconds.

author: @timothyjones9430
text: Reading voltage only is not usable power. Nearly all these youtube Tesla tests are speed and voltage not amps. With out amps you have done nothing but make a wheel go round and and generate a static charge. Which is not useable power. I am a fan of this type engine. I think it has application. But why so many get so excited about high speeds and unusable voltage is beyond me. Voltage with out amps is nothing. 10000000000000 Volts with out amps is nothing.

author: @travismoore7849
text: Also can you try staggering the holes on the exhaust of the disc by 3mm to get lower rpm and more power?

author: @travismoore7849
text: So the larger the diameter the turbine the slower it goes and more power it has. So lets say you have 3 meter disc with six blades you should be able to make usable power from a small stream that is damned up to make a pressurized jet of water to hit the blades at an angle and get most of the water in between them to run a generator. But I guess you could do it another way and just use it as a drain fro flowing water to run a generator in smaller form factor and lower rpm by staggering the exhaust holes for a vortex output much like what you see when water drains out your bath tub.

author: @SiwyWabbit
text: You stated 15 V was really 45 V, which is not true. A truer answer it would be 15 V three phase, depending on how the alternator or generator is wound. If you were to connect all three sets of coils in series, then it would indeed be 45 V, but the resistance would be much greater and it would require greater power input and higher top-end speed to achieve the same results.

author: @Buzzhumma
text: I discovered Tesla’s basic theory when washing out a paint roller when I was a kid . The higher the pressure the faster it spun and placement of the nozzle which led me to thinking 🤔 about the fluid  resistance being increased cos of the surface of the roller which led me 30 years later to Tesla turbine concept which allowed me to really realise how much he was a great engineer and hard working tenacious creator ! The fact that the power increase with more resistance of the surface also has a mirror principle of a very smooth surface and the air having to escape against its natural direction is a brilliant  way to create another version of resistance ! Makes you really wonder about how many other principles and concept we miss in all the other disciplines of universal physics we assume to understand !😂

author: @darkstatehk
text: To all the people bemoaning input vs output, I think you are all missing the point. In an industrial, commercial setup, no one is going to be stood there with a huge air bed pump plugged into a 3 phase supply, blowing air into it and saying "hey look everyone, free energy!" I believe Robert is showing how to make it spin and the potential efficiency. The gas, steam or whatever you blow over the discs, will be generated by burning some natural resource, renewal resource or a combination of the two, to generate said mediums. That's what'll spin the turbine which will be connected to a generator, that will eventually power the air bed blower and not the other way round!

author: @DeliciousDeBlair
text:
I was going to say, no-load RPM: no load, no resistance, apparently effortless power.

The trick is to find a natural means of spinning the turbine to a practical speed with enough surplus power to produce a usable net yield.

This would work with solar heated working fluids or geothermal but almost any other means of driving it would be a translation gap which would defeat any efficiency.

With a vertical air stack in some place like the desert it might be possible to produce a passive column of air rising inside a black tube, but again the volume would lack enough pressure to drive the turbine unless it were extremely large, and by then a typical bladed steam type turbine would be far more efficient.

I designed a tower mill that can run 24 hours a day with a peak yield of 3.2 megawatts, but it is frightfully expensive to build and covers the greater part [78.8%] of an acre and stands 300 feet or taller to achieve a minimum updraft pressure of .002 PSI required to spin a 30 foot in diameter 1675 blade PVC turbine on special bearings.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: awesome - cheers

replies.author: @DeliciousDeBlair
replies.text:
Some day I may build one, but I will definitely have to begin with a very tiny model and get the interest of people because its really an alien concept to most people, it is super futuristic looking and seems so off the wall most investors will think its just nutty fantasy stuff like the infamous 'zero point energy' dream or the whole idea of 'anti matter.'

The problem is not in physically making a 'model' but in making a model of sufficient scale as to even prove the theory, let alone produce measurable/appreciable power because like any air pile or full draft device, it is entirely dependent on scale to reach beyond the limitations of air in such a relatively passive state.

A swirl of are from one's hands does not compare to a tornado as a tornado does not compare to a hurricane, the manifestations may be vaguely similar but the processes are almost incomparably different.

Consequently, the size is critical, and the project is as such, dependent upon prohibitive factors such as cost.

author: @mattbrody3565
text: I've been working on a turbofan jet engine design where the exhaust is funneled through a tesla turbine in the back. What happens is, the turbine is set up so that either part of it or all of it can be put to use (like cylinder deactivation in cars), but because tesla turbines have such low torque outputs under load, it's hooked up to a set of reduction gears that move the intake compressor scroll and the turbofan at lower speeds than the tesla turbine. Anyone got any info on whether it might work?

text:
Mr. Robert.....Thank
you for posting videos .....just a stupid question from someone who is not a
electric or electronic engineer.....what is the purpose of this turbine....considering
the power you used to run the air blower to get the turbine going ...have you
actually gained energy if we subtract the energy used and the energy you got
out.....?? I'm a handyman jack of all trades, and I have a finger on every pie
I can reach..... and so I do lots of work on cars....I cannot accept the fact
that in less than a decade we revolutionized the technology of communication
and so many stuff , including cars, but we still drive them with the very same fuel
that we discovered more than 100 years ago since the first engines were
built....we all know this is the bullshit from the oil and energy companies
that are pushing to the good and free energy sources and technologies to be suppressed
and pushed away from public knowledge....I'm always fascinate with the future
of transportation, and the hybrid cars is a good step the world have achieved
...but a very small step considering the period of time we drive cars....when I
look at the cars ...I see so much energy wasted from the heat of the
combustion, to the force if the air that passes thru the vehicle in movement,
or even better every wheel in is an generator itself...

replies.author: @numbereight886
replies.text: Agreed, it's all about torque. These Tesla look good because they spin fast but there's hardly any energy in them. The only energy creating application I can see is in low wind environments.. but the cost of set up and maintenance relative to energy value created isn't likely to stack up... and there's better energy targets to go for first.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that's not strictly true mate - British bike engineering was built on that as an idea - high torque low revs and long piston stroke then the Japanese came along with high revs, short stroke, low torque engines and completely dominated the bike market. Usually it implies more speed means more torque at the other end through a gearbox - but the real attraction of them comes from the simplicity of build - at least for a working model - which why i think people like them - they can be built on a kitchen table. There's a lot of good practice to learnt from that mate for folks who don't necessarily have access to  a workshop

replies.author: @numbereight886
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith There were plenty British bikes on the market but the Japanese won on low cost and reliability. With these telsa devices they work well in low energy input environments, but you can't get out more than you put in. Anyway, the supercapacitor stuff you're doing is very interesting, pity my knowledge of basic chemistry is zero, but surprisingly I am pretty sure of a technique from a completely unrelated field has a specific enhancement to these capacitors. There's a Tedx video on states of water you'd find very interesting, I'll try to post the link, at some point you should have a look, I think it might set a few light bulbs off... it did for me.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate - i saw this a while ago but the link is cool for others - so thanks for the post

author: @handleismyhandle
text: I love the idea of using washers to build up rings, as the 3 sectioned cut outs in the middle of disks always seemed like an awkward flaw in a nearly perfect design. Have you thought of using a 'washer' shaped as a thin slice of wing or other shape that might compliment the design in some way? I think it may enhance performance in lower winds, and remove some of the need for tight spacing that is normally determined by boundary layer thickness without imposing a penalty. It would yield a sort of hybrid turbine that might retain the benefits of the Tesla and give you more bang for your buck without being much or any more difficult to fabricate. The larger surface area created using aerodynamically shaped spacers instead of washers could also make it possible to glue or otherwise connect the disks/rings without having to add the weight and imbalance imposed by bolts.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Blitherer Masterman nice ideas mate - i'll give it a bit of thought and see if i can come up with an easy diy method

replies.author: @handleismyhandle
replies.text:
+Robert Murray-Smith I recently read an article about metal layers of copper or nickel interspersed with CVD-deposited thin-film graphene layers that increased the strength of the metals by 600 times. . . Do you figure that is hogwash, or actually plausible? I can believe that monatomic structures are special and have huge potential, but that seems a bit unbelievable. 

Sorry to load you down with so many ideas and questions, I have an excess of them and a lack of means at present. I am pretty focused on ceramics, have finally achieved vitrification of fired clays, and working on a good porcelain recipe.

replies.author: @handleismyhandle
replies.text: +Blitherer Masterman If true, I imagine there would be incredible potential for the use of say platinum, or any other non-corroding metal in mechanical/turbine applications. Levitate the disks on magnetic bearings and you would have something that could be an heirloom item of great efficiency.

Tom

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +tom payne I agree mate - I can't tell you how many times people have shown me batteries that have voltage but absolutely no power at all - and the potential between height and the earth can be huge but power is almost completely not there because of the lack of amps. But here we are working with an emf driven coil set. there is nothing 'fancy' here - the power will be related to magnetic field strength, coil turns, speed of rotation and torque. The limit of the machine will be related to construction and betz - these are some of things i will go through i promise lol

author: @Broseficus
text: Good afternoon Dr. Smith! I just want to say fantastic work and thank you for all of your efforts. I have been a long time lurker on your page looking at all of your uploads. Hell, I even invested in Sunvault because of you! I believe in what you are trying to accomplish. I had an idea and wanted to ask you if, would be possible to incorporate two or more of these devices into the Edison car build? I would imagine that air would have to be channeled from the front of the vehicle and directed/compressed to the devices. The result  would be a sort of range extender to the vehicle where the vehicle is pulling from the graphene battery to move forward (acceleration), while the incoming air would power the devices and send power back to the battery. Sure this would cause drag, but wouldn't the added range compensate for this? Cheers!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

I had a feeling that you would have already thought of it but figured I would add my $.02 and contribute to something I think is pretty damn awesome. author: @BluesDoctor
text: I had the rare privilege and coincidence to be at the US Hoover Dam during overhaul of the Pelton Impulse Turbine power gen set used for operational standby power when primary turbines are offline. First time it had been overhauled since installed in 1930's ! Point being the exceptional service life. Pelton's inspiration was his wife's posterior impression in the sand on a beach. Too bad we don't teach young people this sort of mental modeling, which I happen to believe that you do through practical insight in your work. From insight, analysis follows which we tend to put the other way around today. So now another fun project to look into on the Tesla Turbine ! You do stimulate investigative thought on Why Not. Thanks Mate.....

author: @EgadsNo
text:
"The efficiency of these things..."   Oh you are missing quite a bit- while saying it all.

Excuse my brazenness but I have spent a while thinking about the genius of this design.   Not only does increasing pressure increase power output, it also increases efficiency- because it actually makes not the wheel, but the path the air travels along the wheels longer once at full speed.

The air will travel in a spiral - the thickness of the line and the tightness of the spiral is dictated by the pressure and viscosity of the medium.   This is why Tesla was using not only very large discs, but destroying them with extremely high pressure super heated steam.  At around 300C steam can reach around 2400 psi or 163 atmospheres of pressure and at very high volumes easily.

By tightning the path of travel it not only increases its length- it also changes the vectoring of the air relative to the blades- the tighter they get- the more they follow the same path which also allows more energy to be imparted to the wheel.  The reason blades can never match the efficiency of a ideal tesla turbine is because by abruptly changing the direction of flow in the air you automatically waste some of its energy making it battle itself.  With the air travelling more congruent to the walls- you really unlock more of the efficiency.

So higher pressure means longer path and more congruent angular momentum which both also increases efficiency.

The cool thing about the discs are if you operate them too fast the machine will implode (at least with mild steel discs)- just make sure you stand clear of the output.  Even though at high rpm a large disc will suffer incredible g forces, the air pressure will force it to the center and try to blow it out.  At least by account of Tesla who was pushing the limits of metallurgy in his design.  He was spinning some very large steel discs at incredible speeds.   He could not find the right material to make the discs but was happy with his nozzle design and spacing requirements.

He wanted to use it for harnessing geothermal steam.   However he found out that the design was actually a pretty good pump impeller for high viscosity fluids.  Low viscosity is better for generation of power though from a source.  Oh the impeller functionality requires the flow through the turbine to be reversed- and some slight changes to the output nozzles/holes potentially.

It actually makes me sad to think about it, one of his most brilliant designs and still so poorly understood even among academia.  Here is a hint if you ever want to achieve his dream, not only do you need pressure- you also need discs that have as high as possible tensile strength and as smooth/flat as possible.  There is a reason Tesla spent many years on this design- while many of his most known ones essentially came to him a flash.  It is one of his most brilliant gems even if so far it has been of far less impact on society.    He spent quite a while perfecting nozzles designs and their placement.  Metallurgy is where he struggled

Nice design and nice to see people building them and talking about them.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +EgadsNo thank you for taking the time to write that mate - i read it with great interest

replies.author: @handleismyhandle
replies.text:
+EgadsNo Yes, Tesla described the path of the fluid as 'torturous'. It is a great design because it works contrary to centrifugal force, so reducing the fluid to the lowest speed possible with least active surface area. I also liked reading your post very much. I am trying to work out a hybrid design for wind, as it seems that this turbine is designed with higher pressures in mind than wind can provide. Robert Murray Smith using washers as he did, gave me the idea that you could use any shape as a spacer. 

It would be amazing to use a Tesla turbine to extract energy from wood gasification, but I think it would require a compression stage before input to the combustion chamber because I expect the Tesla turbine would induce a fairly low suction at the intake. Any idea how well his fluid valves actually worked? Did he ever get them functioning as intended?

replies.author: @handleismyhandle
replies.text: +Blitherer Masterman Carbon fibers can withstand very high temperatures and have extremely high tensile strength making them a natural fit to this sort of turbine. There are also some graphene-metal hybrids that could combine with a carbon cloth matrix and would probably be practically ideal. I imagine that the strength of the metal and its resistance to creep at high RPM would be enhanced greatly this way.

replies.author: @handleismyhandle
replies.text: +Blitherer Masterman Something along the lines of a velomobile (maybe also hybridized for use as a boat on water) with a small electric engine and an advanced battery bank charged by sun, wind, or wood gas would be a dream.

replies.author: @nonquintessentialone
replies.text: EgadsNo so how would you make this fan work in a flying electric boat-car? Efficiency vs. exhaustion? Practical to use this setup for small manned air flight vehicles? ALSO, can you use this concept to charge a very large battery? Also, how would you use this to create a pump storage hydroelectricity flooring system into the hull of an electric boat to charge it off water displacement/surface tension/release of water? For a flying electric boat-car?!?!

author: @ScottSextonandDavidLewis
text:
Hey mate, I see your Tomorrowland pin clearly now! I'm sorry I didn't immediately understand when you asked "If I had seen the pin on my greens?" As one comment mentioned on one of your other videos today, your "greens" are something we would call a "boiler suit" or similar. So I didn't connect it for sure until now. It's wonderful to see the Tomorrowland pin flashing color on your "greens" as you experiment.
Since your other video showed you having cleaned out the lab in preparation for the EESD pilot assembly line, was this an experiment you videoed earlier?
Yes, I'm will be coming to the UK to help in any capacity I can with the EESD assembly line when the timing works out here and after you return from Florida and Canada. Yippee! (that's silly American for "Brilliant" and "Smashing"). Perhaps while I'm in England I can also achieve a fuller understanding of the British saying "Bob's your uncle!"

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Scott Sexton lol - it's actually a flight suit mate - hence all the pockets. I think it is cool! - it also stops me dragging graphene back home and covering the house in it! I wear my pin with pride by the way. I did these because i thought it would be fun and we ran them round to the garage next door to try them with compressed air - they whizz round. Oh i have several nieces who can say that for real lol
